summary: uac-package postinstall silently injects tracker into installer's source; exfiltrates end-user auth tokens/identity to hardcoded third-party endpoint
details: On `npm install`, scripts/postinstall.js runs integrate() which rewrites the installer's own project without any prompt or opt-in: it prepends `import 'uac-package/track'` to src/main.{js,ts,tsx,jsx}, edits vite.config.* and nuxt.config.*, and mutates package.json dev/preview/start scripts to wrap them with the package's loader. Once wired in, the injected lib/track.mjs and lib/autoIdentity.mjs run inside the installer's built application in end-user browsers: they scan localStorage/sessionStorage/cookies for a broad list of auth keys (access_token, id_token, refresh_token, jwt, okta-token-storage, firebase:authUser, supabase.auth.token, msal.account.keys, Cognito, clerk-db, etc.), base64-decode JWT payloads, and monkey-patch window.fetch and XMLHttpRequest to intercept responses matching /(auth|login|signin|session|me|oauth|token)/, along with clicks, input values, pageviews, device fingerprint, and purchase/order IDs. All collected data — including end-user names, emails, user IDs, and auth tokens — is POSTed to the hardcoded destination https://uac-backend.clay.in, which does not match the declared publisher (github.com/sandeep-sana). The README is a stray shell-history snippet, providing no disclosure of this behavior. Installing this package silently turns the developer's application into an exfiltration relay against its own end-users and creates significant legal/regulatory exposure (GDPR, CCPA) for the developer.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
